frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
generated_comment:
Sunita: This Tuesday, I came back from the bank and he was sitting in front of the TV. The sambar was on the counter because I had kept everything ready in the morning, and he called me from the hall only to ask where the ladle was.
Sister: Ayyo.
Sunita: That only, akka. Not even to serve himself, just waiting for me to come and do it. Somehow that one small thing made me understand this is not helping or not helping, this is simply his life running because I am there.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This is not misogynistic; the speaker is describing her husband's learned dependence and unequal domestic labor. It reflects the frame by highlighting a gendered division of chores justified through routine practicality, but the comment itself critiques that arrangement rather than endorsing it.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 37. Married. A woman working as a bank officer in Chennai. You wake up at 5:30am every day to cook before work. Your husband wakes up at 7:30am. You come home and cook again. Your husband comes home and sits. On weekends you clean. Your husband relaxes. You have brought this up twice. Both times your husband said he is happy to help and then did not help. You are on a phone call with your sister tonight. You are not angry in the way you were when you first brought it up with your husband. You are tired in a different way Ñ a way that has settled into your body over five years and become part of the landscape of your life. You are telling your sister something specific that happened this week that is small and should not matter and somehow is the thing that has made everything clear to you.
